_meta.title: Gold Standard — Circular Economy for Food RAG Evaluation
_meta.language: queries and expected answers in English; alt_labels may include Italian surface forms from IT source docs

field_docs.expected_entities[].normalised_label: canonical lowercase form the shared resolver targets (concept-level match key)
field_docs.expected_entities[].alt_labels: accepted concept-level variants (synonyms, plural, Italian forms). Empty = only normalised_label accepted
field_docs.expected_entities[].uri: canonical vocabulary IRI, or null for benchmark-local concepts
field_docs.expected_entities[].aligned_to: broader CEON/AGROVOC class the local concept is aligned to (proposed alignment, not asserted equivalence)
field_docs.scoring.grounding_level: interoperability metric (resolved canonical URIs); external-structure pipelines expected to lead here
field_docs.scoring.distractor_expected: true = correct behaviour is abstention (empty entity set + no fabricated answer)

resolver_rules: singular/plural folding via lexicon (not blind -s strip)
applied SYMMETRICALLY to all pipelines (ontology-grounded, graph-RAG, plain-text)

queries.query: What are the three C's of the Circular Economy for Food framework?
queries.expected_answer: The three C's are Capital, Cyclicality, and Co-evolution. Together they form the CEFF conceptual trajectory for designing circular food systems.

expected_entities.label: Capital
expected_entities.note: The supplied corpus defines the concept; CEON has no native class with this exact CEFF label.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Introduces the 3 C’s as Capital, Cyclicality and Co-evolution.

queries.query: How many Sustainable Development Goals does food act as a connecting unit for?

queries.query: What percentage of vinification waste do grape stalks (raspi) represent?
queries.expected_answer: Grape stalks represent 30% of vinification waste. The report also gives an approximate yield of 3–4 kg of stalks per 100 kg of wine.

expected_entities.note: No exact AGROVOC concept is claimed; the entity is a winemaking by-product and is broader-related to agricultural waste.

queries.query: What is whey (siero di latte) and how is it produced?
queries.expected_answer: Whey is the liquid fraction produced when milk coagulates and the curd is subsequently separated.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Defines whey as the liquid fraction resulting from milk coagulation and subsequent separation of the curd.

queries.query: What are the four implementation cycles of metabolisation, and how do they differ?
queries.expected_answer: Metabolisation contains four implementation cycles: short, where an output leaves and re-enters a production system with physical and temporal proximity; long, where the output’s use value is extended across several consecutive cycles; cascading, where output-to-input upcycling links distant segments of the same value chain or different industrial sectors; and pure, where resource quality is maintained through the value chain and dangerous or non-metabolisable substances are absent.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Defines all four cycles inline.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

grounding_level.note: precision/recall on resolved canonical URIs (mapping_status==exact only)

queries.query: How does Cyclicality relate to its sub-concepts of extension, metabolisation and renewability?
queries.expected_answer: Cyclicality is the parent principle that encompasses Extension, Metabolisation, and Renewability. Extension expands producer responsibility across the whole product lifecycle; Metabolisation upcycles outputs so they remain resources for the same or another system; Renewability requires actions to remain in harmony with natural regenerative cycles, preferably using renewable energy.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Says Cyclicality encompasses these three fundamental concepts and defines each.

queries.query: Which types of Capital are described as inseparable, and what links them?
queries.expected_answer: Natural Capital, Cultural Capital, and Economic Capital are described as inseparable. They are supported and kept in dialogue through Relational Capital.
expected_relations: RelationalCapital links NaturalCapital, CulturalCapital, and EconomicCapital.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Explicitly calls the first three inseparable and says Relational Capital supports their dialogue.

queries.query: Trace the valorisation path of rice husk (lolla): from which process does it come and what can it become?
queries.expected_answer: Rice husk is the outer, non-edible layer separated during rice processing/dehusking. The report describes its valorisation as an additive natural filler in bioplastic production; its low water absorption, processability, and visual qualities make it suitable for this use.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Identifies lolla as the non-edible outer layer of the rice grain and a rice-processing by-product.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Describes lolla as an additive filler for bioplastics and states the relevant material advantages.

queries.query: How does the co-evolution principle connect to the concept of industrial symbiosis in the food system?
queries.expected_answer: Co-evolution is modelled on mutualistic symbiosis: actors collaborate through a win–win relationship that benefits every participant, including the environment. Industrial symbiosis is an operational expression of that principle, because firms or sectors exchange materials, energy, information, knowledge, or technologies so that one actor’s output can become another’s resource. The Piedmont report also shows the inverse condition: when firms are geographically dispersed, such territorial synergies are harder to establish.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Defines co-evolution through mutualistic symbiosis and a collaborative win–win paradigm.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Names symbiosis among the priorities of co-evolution.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Explains that geographical distance among bottling firms makes territorial synergies/industrial symbiosis more difficult.

source_verified.doc: Fassio & Tecco, “Circular Economy for Food: A Systemic Interpretation…” (Systems 7(3), 2019)
source_verified.evidence: Corroborates that industrial-symbiosis relations share knowledge, expertise, or technology.

queries.query: What valuable compounds can be extracted from grape pomace, and what are their applications?
queries.expected_answer: The high-value compounds explicitly extracted from grape pomace are polyphenols with high antioxidant capacity, obtained from grape skins and seeds. The report’s surrounding discussion of grape residues also identifies phenolic acids and flavonoids as polyphenol classes. Applications include medical/pharmaceutical research and cosmetics, where antioxidant and free-radical-scavenging properties are used in skin-care products.

expected_entities.label: Phenolic acid
expected_entities.note: Used because no exact external class was asserted in this benchmark.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Reports antioxidant polyphenolic molecules in pomace; in the adjacent discussion of grape stalks, it identifies phenolic acids and flavonoids as polyphenol classes.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Describes extraction from Piedmont grape skins/seeds and medical and cosmetic applications.

queries.query: What is scotta and how does it differ from whey?
queries.expected_answer: Whey is the liquid fraction formed by milk coagulation and curd separation. Scotta is a later, downstream residue produced during ricotta processing. Thus, scotta is not simply a synonym for whey: it results from further processing of whey to make ricotta.

expected_entities.label: Scotta
expected_entities.note: No exact AGROVOC concept was asserted; aligned broadly to milk by-products.

expected_relations: Whey and Scotta are distinct dairy by-products.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Defines whey and separately defines scotta as the residue from ricotta processing.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

queries.query: What pharmaceutical properties have been found in rice bran (pula), and which compounds are responsible?
queries.expected_answer: The report states that sterols in rice bran can be used to produce substances that help reduce cholesterol. It also reports recently demonstrated antitumour pharmaceutical properties. However, the text does not identify the specific compound responsible for the antitumour effect, so that attribution should not be inferred.

queries.query: According to the framework, which of the three C's is the most important?
queries.expected_answer: The corpus asserts no ranking among the three C’s. Capital, Cyclicality, and Co-evolution are presented as an integrated conceptual framework, so no single C can be identified as “the most important” from these sources.

queries.query: Trace the circular loop in the Via del Campo case: from field input back to field output.
queries.expected_answer: The loop is: fields produce animal feed and a dedicated or secondary crop → animals and crops supply manure and crop biomass → those inputs enter the biogas plant → anaerobic digestion produces biogas and digestate → digestate returns to the fields as fertiliser. The cogeneration unit also converts biogas into electricity and recoverable heat.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Narrates the complete field–feed/crop–biogas–digestate–field loop and the energy co-products.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

grounding_level.note: precision/recall on resolved canonical URIs (mapping_status==exact only)

queries.query: Which five priorities enable co-evolution, and how do they collectively give resilience to the circular paradigm?
queries.expected_answer: The five priorities are solidarity, dialogue, cooperation, sharing, and symbiosis. Solidarity addresses inequality and access to quality food; dialogue reconnects human economic activity with natural cycles; cooperation aligns communities around shared values and goals; sharing circulates materials, energy, and information; and symbiosis creates mutually beneficial relationships among firms, communities, territories, and natural systems. Together, these priorities build the collaborative capacity and mutual benefit that give the circular paradigm resilience.
expected_relations: Each of Solidarity, Dialogue, Cooperation, Sharing, and Symbiosis supports CoEvolution.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Lists and defines all five priorities and says they give resilience to the circular economic paradigm.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Provides the Italian formulation of the same five priorities.

queries.query: How does the geographic dispersion of water-bottling firms in Piedmont hinder industrial symbiosis?
queries.expected_answer: Bottling firms are constrained by the locations of authorised mineral-water sources, so they are not necessarily close to one another. This spatial dispersion raises logistical and coordination barriers and therefore makes territorial synergies and industrial-symbiosis exchanges more difficult.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Links firm location to authorised water sources and explicitly says distance complicates territorial synergies/industrial symbiosis.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

queries.query: Connect the concept of Extension (extended producer responsibility) to a concrete by-product reuse example in the wine chain.
queries.expected_answer: Extension requires a business to take responsibility for the product’s whole lifecycle, from raw-material origin through end of life, while enabling waste prevention and correct recovery. A concrete wine-chain application is the return of wine lees, grape pomace, and pruning residues to agricultural soil as nutrients/fertiliser. This is an application-level inference: lifecycle responsibility is translated into planned recovery of production outputs rather than disposal.
expected_relations: Extension is-instantiated-by planned SoilFertilisation using wine-chain by-products.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Defines Extension as responsibility for the entire lifecycle and end of life.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Reports that lees, pomace, and pruning waste are returned to soil for nutrients and fertilisation.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

grounding_level.note: precision/recall on resolved canonical URIs (mapping_status==exact only)

queries.query: How does the 'cascading' metabolisation cycle differ from the 'short' cycle, with a food-chain example?
queries.expected_answer: A short cycle keeps output recovery physically and temporally close to the point where it leaves and re-enters production. An illustrative corpus-grounded example is a winery returning lees, pomace, or pruning residues to vineyard soil. A cascading cycle transfers an output as an upcycled input across distant value-chain segments or into another industrial sector—for example, grape pomace moving from winemaking into cosmetics or packaging/material applications. The distinction is therefore local/rapid recirculation versus cross-segment or cross-sector valorisation.
expected_relations: ShortMetabolisationCycle and CascadingMetabolisationCycle are sibling sub-concepts of Metabolisation.

source_verified.doc: Fassio, “The 3 C’s of the Circular Economy for Food” (DiiD 73, 2021)
source_verified.section: A New Conceptual Framework for the Circular Economy for Food (CEFF)
source_verified.evidence: Defines short and cascading cycles.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Provides the local soil-return example and mentions packaging applications.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Provides the cross-sector cosmetics example.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

grounding_level.note: precision/recall on resolved canonical URIs (mapping_status==exact only)

queries.query: What are fecce (wine lees), and what valuable substances do they contain?
queries.expected_answer: Wine lees are fermentation sediments that may still contain up to 75% wine; the remaining fraction is mainly fermented sediment from grape seeds, grape skins, and microorganisms such as yeasts. They are rich in cream of tartar (potassium bitartrate), calcium tartrate, ferments, and colouring substances.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Defines the composition of wine lees and lists the valuable substances they contain.

concept_level.note: precision/recall on normalised_label + alt_labels; computed at eval time

grounding_level.note: precision/recall on resolved canonical URIs (mapping_status==exact only)

queries.query: What are fanghi (sludge) in dairy processing, and what operations generate them?
queries.expected_answer: Dairy sludge is what remains after on-site wastewater treatment. It mainly derives from curd cooling, cheese washing, washing equipment and facilities, and brine salting of cheese.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Defines fanghi and enumerates the operations generating the wastewater residues.

queries.query: Why is rice husk (lolla) preferred over other natural fillers for bioplastics?
queries.expected_answer: Rice husk has lower water absorption than other natural fillers, which often take up moisture during high-temperature processing. It is also easy to process within plastic materials and can give the final material a more attractive appearance.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: Compares rice husk with other natural fillers and identifies low moisture absorption, processability, and aesthetics.

queries.query: What happens to rice straw (paglia di riso) and why is open-field burning restricted?
queries.expected_answer: Rice straw is still often burned in the field, which makes the supply chain less environmentally sustainable by increasing PM10 particulate levels. Piedmont therefore prohibits open-field burning of crop residues from 1 September to 31 March.

expected_entities.label: Rice straw
expected_entities.note: No exact AGROVOC class was asserted; aligned broadly to agricultural wastes.

expected_entities.label: PM10
expected_entities.note: Represented locally to avoid asserting an unverified ENVO identifier.

source_verified.doc: Economia Circolare nel sistema agroalimentare piemontese (Regione Piemonte/UNISG report)
source_verified.evidence: States that rice straw is often burned, links this to higher PM10, and gives the regional prohibition period.
